sentences.text: My current name is Charlotte Seeman, and I was born Charlotte Marasch Leiter.
I was born in Vienna, Austria, on November 17th, 1920.
Not in Poland, in Vienna.
I never was in Poland.
When my parents came to Austria, they wanted to become a citizen.
In order to be citizen, you had to bring papers.
So they brought the marriage license, but the marriage license was in Yiddish.
They never went to a court or anything.
They were only married by the rabbi.
So Austria didn't recognize that.
According to this, my father never married my mother.
So we all were kids out of wedlock, according to the law.
So we had to change our name to my mother's parents'.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So my-- [INAUDIBLE] it went back, and they changed the name from Leiter-- that was my father's name-- to Marasch, what was probably one of the grandparents' names.
So we changed the name.
But we were known always under the name Leiter.
Tell me a little about your childhood.
I was born in Vienna in an Orthodox home.
My parents believed in the synagogue.
My father used to go every Shabbos to shul.
And Friday night, we lit the candles, we did all that, what the Jewish people lived.
We lived in the Jewish district, what was like-- not like a ghetto, but in a very Jewish neighborhood, where all our neighbors were Jewish.
And that's how I grew up.
I had one brother who was 14 years older than I am, and I had one sister who was seven years older than I am.
I was a child that was not supposed to be there, so I was born much later.
I was the baby of the family.
I was a very sick child all the years that I can remember in my childhood.
I hardly went to street or out because I was always sick.
I started school when I was 7 years old, and then they took my tonsils out and I became better.
I improved in health.
As a child, I was a very sickly child.
I was very undernourished.
I was very skinny as a child.
And now you can see me, that I changed.
Now I have a hard time to keep away from the food.
My mother was [INAUDIBLE].
That was her maiden name.
She was a seamstress.
My father used to work in [INAUDIBLE]-- I don't know how to translate it-- in hair from the pigs.
My father had the slaughterhouses, rented.
Everything that-- hair that came off was his.
We had places where they throw that out, like fields, and people used to gather that in baskets to assort it by different qualities.
We had also, I remember, a factory where we had ovens that were used to wash that and dye it in colors, and bundle it up according to the categories.
And my father used to sell that wholesale to the people who worked with the people who make brushes.
I had a brother.
His name was Israel Moses, called Izzy Leiter.
And I had a sister whose name was Peppi, and she got married with a man.
His name was [?
Alfred ?] [?
Rein. ?] As a child, I went to school, to a public school.
And later on, I went to private school.
The private schools was named [INAUDIBLE], where I learned bookkeeping and English and typing and shorthand and all that kind of thing, like for bookkeeping.
I attend cheder.
And I went into school only two years, and I didn't finish either the second year, because we were not allowed to attend the school anymore.
This was not a Jewish school, and Jewish children were not allowed to attend it later on when Hitler came.
That was in 1938.
When Hitler came in, we were not allowed to go there no more.
So after that-- before that, already, I used to go to work also by my brother-in-law.
And he had a wholesale place in clothing.
And I worked there part-time.
And I went to school at the same time.
Who were your friends when you were in school?
I had mostly Jewish friends because I lived in the Jewish district and I had not much contact with not Jewish children.
I belonged to the Mizrahi as a child, what was a Jewish organization for mostly Orthodox.
And in our house where I lived was mostly Orthodox Jewish.
We had very few gentile tenants.
I lived in an apartment house, four flights, and like I say, mostly Jewish people.
Maybe there was two or three non-Jewish in the apartment house.
It was never good, because no matter how you talk about it, it was always anti-Semitic.
I had a very religious mother who used to always give because she believed that we have to give in order to receive.
And when I used to question my mother, why do you give this [INAUDIBLE], he's a drunk, and so.
But she always used to tell me, it's not your question to ask.
God will judge, and nott-- we are not allowed to judge.
You have to give.
And if he is a drunk, he is punished enough.
That was my mother's politic.
And that we were not religious enough.
My mother went to Poland-- one of the few-- one of the latest that went from Vienna away.
She went with very prominent people because that-- were kept till 1942 in Vienna.
She went to [INAUDIBLE], near Lublin.
She wrote me one postal card, and wrote me that she had nothing to eat and nothing to wear, and I should send with-- I went to my brother, who lived with me in Amsterdam.
We sent money, we sent clothing, we sent food.
We never heard from my mother anymore.
We got the postal card back from the Jewish [INAUDIBLE], that was the head of the committee there, that the woman, Mrs.
Leiter, Marasch, she is not more present.
So my mother was taken away, and we heard she was killed there, where they killed them alive.
They buried them alive.
So I never saw my mother anymore.
I went away.
I was 18 years old.
I went away, and after that, I never saw my parents anymore.
I want to talk about when things started to change for you in Vienna.
What was the first indications that things were going really wrong?
I remember the day when Hitler came in.
He went to the main street in Vienna.
I was coming home from school.
And I had-- the school was in the first district, and we lived in the second.
It was very scary.
The people were screaming and hollering.
And when he drove through, the people became hysterical.
They were very much in favor of the Anschluss-- that means the connection with them.
And as a Jew, it was not very popular at the time to be in the street.
I rushed home as fast as I could to be at my house.
After that, it changed.
Day by day, it changed.
We were not allowed to go to school anymore.
We couldn't get anything we wanted anymore.
And I decided-- I went to the Kristallnacht.
I remember that very clearly.
I remember how we had neighbors that were standing-- had to stand on a box or on a chair, and they were pulling on his beard.
His name was [?
Krupnik ?].
And he had a grocery store, and how they tortured them.
My father was also tortured.
We had the place what I said, the factory out of town, because that what my father did was not allowed in Vienna.
And many times, they took my father and put him in prison there.
My father had cancer and he was on medication, and my mother used to go there and beg that they should let them have at least the medication.
My father died before they could take him away, and I think that this was part that prolonged-- made it faster that he died.
He had a lot of shame when that happened, and it didn't do him any good.
I remember the last day.
My father was laying in the bed because I left very early in the morning.
He blessed me, and that was the last time I saw any of them.
[SOBBING] I didn't see my mother or my father afterwards no more.
I was only 18.
I went on foot to Holland.
I drove till Cologne, and then I walked over the border with three more people.
And we came to a very small town.
I went with one of them into the hotel, and we asked that we were just coming over the border, if could stay there.
And the-- I said somebody will come from Amsterdam to pick us up.
While we were in the hotel, the two other ones was picked up and we never heard from them no more.
They were shipped back to Germany.
So I was with him that was the uncle, an older person, older than me, and we two survived.
And somebody came from Amsterdam and picked us both up.
Who came from Amsterdam to pick you up?
I had a boyfriend who lived in Amsterdam, and he came and picked me up.
Absolutely.
He told me how to go.
We went to the city.
The name was Venlo in Holland.
We went first to Germany, to Cologne.
Then we took a little train to the border, and in there, we went on foot the whole night.
It was December 12th.
It was snow and everything.
It was very cold, and we walked to foot most of the night.
And we couldn't identify where we were, because on this point comes Belgium and Holland and Germany together.
It is a three-point.
But one of the girls who spoke French said, we are not in Holland.
We are in Belgium.
So we walked back and forth and back and forth till we came to Venlo.
The lights was above us, and the [GERMAN] from the German.
And we came to Venlo and we asked them where that restaurant or hotel, whatever it was.
We went in there to wait.
And the two other ones were outside, but they were picked up and we never heard from them.
I don't know where they went.
I went with the uncle to Amsterdam.
I had my brother at the time living with his wife and her mother in Amsterdam, so I moved in with my family.
I'm beginning from '39.
When I got to Amsterdam, I lived with my brother.
He took me up to the committee because we were not allowed to work.
The committee gave me a piece of paper to go to the police, and I had to sign that I never will work because there was too many unemployment.
We were not allowed to work.
And I had to come every week to the police to sign that I didn't work.
The committee gave us money to live.
We got $6-- guilder and 25 cent, what barely makes [? it ?] go by.
But everything was cheap, and we were four together, so we made a go of it.
Like I say, my boyfriend never survived.
He was picked up and he never came back.
I was with his parents together.
Also, they were picked up, and they didn't come back.
What event can I tell you?
I went to a dance, and my husband was sitting there.
And my husband was talking that his parents will come from Vienna, and that he had a smuggler.
And my brother was sitting next to me with my sister-in-law, and we were very much interested in that, because I still had my mother there.

And we wanted to know who brought-- who will bring the parents to Amsterdam, and if we can meet my husband at the-- then so we can have the name of the man that will bring him, because we wanted to bring our mother.
So my husband said, yes, you can come to me, and he gave me the name and address.
And if my mother and my father going to come, you can hire the man and he will bring your mother.
But what happened is that when my in-laws came over the border, they lived in Cologne, and it was a very poor hotel, because the committee there in Cologne gave it to them.
It was cold, it was winter, it was icy, and she slipped down the steps and got killed.
So she never came to Holland.
She was buried in Cologne, and my father-in-law came by himself.
At the time, it became worse and worse, and we couldn't bring my mother no more over.
It was too late.
So my mother was later picked up and sent to Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's how it is.
My husband's name is Abraham Seeman.
I married in 1940, in September 11th.
It was already Hitler.
My mother still sent me the paper because at the time, you were not allowed to marry out of your religion.
So my mother wrote me down that I was Jewish.
She went also in Vienna to the [INAUDIBLE] who gave the paper that we were both 100% Jewish so we could marry.
Because it was not allowed no more- no intermarriages or anything.
I married with my husband, and I'm married now for 55 years.
But my brother was picked up.
So was his wife and her mother, and none of them survived.
My brother lived-- they told me that he lived by the liberation.
He went to the death march, and somehow he got killed or he-- whatever, he was shot or something.
My brother never came.
I had already my apartment where I lived in Amsterdam, and we prepared for him.
I know that she was not living, because a friend of mine came back that knew my brother and my sister-in-law, and said that my sister-in-law was selected for the gas chamber.
So she knew that she didn't come, but she said to me the whole time, your brother is doing very well.
He's working for the German, because my brother was  a tailor, and a very, very good tailor.
And he made uniforms for the German army, for these officers there in Auschwitz.
And they told me he was doing very well, and he will come.
But he never came.
He never survived it, the march.
Like they told me in the march, they had no shoes, they had no clothing, they had no food.
Probably something happened to him.
They were all very young.
My brother was in his 30s, my sister-in-law was in her 20s.
So young people, but none of them came back.
People got-- we got a restriction that after 8 o'clock, we were not allowed to go in the street.
That's when the [INAUDIBLE] started, after 8 o'clock in the evening.
Everybody had to be in the house so it was easy for them to pick us up.
They came with papers.
I remember very well.
They came one night, and they knocked at our door.
My husband went to the door and opened up, and before we could do anything, they had the foot right in the door so we couldn't close no more.
And they asked for a name that wasn't our name.
It was the neighbors on the second floor.
So we told them, and we showed them the paper that we-- my husband has a permit to stay.
So they went upstairs.
Upstairs was a family with three small children.
A man and a wife and three infants.
Maybe one was 6 months old, one was a year and a half, and one was 2 and a half.
The screaming is not to describe.
They were middle in the night.
They came and picked up the three children.
So they had to dress them, and they were screaming, hurry, hurry, hurry because there was not too much time.
And that's how they went away.
I never saw anyone no more.
No.
No.
At the time, we all was told that we go to labor camp.
So we had no idea that it was-- from Auschwitz, we never knew.
From gas chamber, we never heard of.
So we thought, being young people, we will survive.
Maybe the older not, going to the camps.
They're going to be too hard for them or they wouldn't have no food.
But for the younger, we didn't hesitate.
We always thought we will survive.
Young people in their 20s-- we expected that we will survive it.
But none of mine-- very few of my friends came back.
You know that in Amsterdam, most of them didn't survive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very few.
We were, after Poland, in Amsterdam, the city that lost in percentage the most people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And Dutch people were not used like Polish, to hunger or to starvation [INAUDIBLE].
They never had the war.
They were always living comfortable.
They had no idea what was going on.
Also not we.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The first time that we heard from Auschwitz was when we were in hiding.
And we were in the attic, and Jan Kloek had a brother who volunteered to go for the German and became a Nazi.
He was on furlough, and he came, and he told Jan what was going on there, from Auschwitz and from the gas chamber.
But we couldn't believe it.
We couldn't believe that something like that in the twentieth century existed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We couldn't [INAUDIBLE] imagine something like that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Germans who were cultures of high standing should do a thing like that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know what else I can tell you.
Which family?
I have no idea what happened to them.
They were supposed to go-- we had a big show, like a theater.
All of the people that were picked up at night went to this theater.
And there was Jewish people who handled them, under the restriction from the German, actually.
And they were sent away.
They went to a camp, to a mess camp-- not into town, out of town.
And from there, they went to Poland with the trains.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know what happened to all these people.
They were probably with children, so they went right to the gas chambers.
No.
I never-- after that, I went away from home.
I was not, like, with my parents anymore.
I started questioning, you know?
Why should that happen to my mother?
My mother was a very precious woman, always very religious, always giving and giving and giving.
And I asked myself the question, why should anything happen to my mother?
She wasn't the person that it should happen to.
And I started asking.
When I asked that in Vienna to my mother-- we didn't know from Auschwitz or anything, but we knew that we were sent away.
My mother used to always say, don't ask questions.
That's how God wants it.
She always put herself down that that was-- we weren't religious enough, we didn't observe the Shabbos and the holidays.
And she-- you're not allowed to question.
God questions.
That was his way of dealing with us.
But I could not put myself down with that.
I asked myself all of these questions.
By the way, my brother, who was not religious, became very religious before he was picked up.
He hired the man, he sat [INAUDIBLE] constantly, and he became very religious.
When he went away, the main thing what he wanted to take was his tallis and his tefillin and his siddur.
He went back what my mother used to teach him.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
My brother was picked up?
I was hiding already.
I went to my brother.
My brother was very, very nervous and sick.
In the evening, when it became to 8 o'clock, he became hysterical.
He was scared.
He was very, very scared.
He ran a fever.
He was at time in the hospital.
Then he came out-- he was very frightened to go away.
And that's when he became so religious.
He held himself that God should help him.
We met the people.
We had a store, and we lived in the back.
And we had, like, a [INAUDIBLE] there.
When the English came over, we had, like, the lights shining, the beams shining, on the airplane because they wanted to shoot it down.
When you shoot it down, little pieces of metal fall down, and people were hiding in order not to get caught with that.
And this young couple stood in our [INAUDIBLE] to hide for that till that's going to go over, and then they continue.
So my husband, who was always very interested when the English came-- we were happy to see it-- were outside, too.
So he met the people and he said, why don't you come inside and have a cup of tea?
So they came inside, and that's how we met them.
They sat inside there.
We talked about-- the woman was pregnant.
And we said, why should you stay outside?
You might as well sit with us.
And the man came back and back and back.
